topic: Areas of Inquiry: Music for Listening and Performance
subject: music

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: Whose 1953 Concentus Musicus Wien revived historically informed Baroque playing?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nikolaus Harnoncourt. “John Eliot Gardiner” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Christopher Small. “Susan McClary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Glenn Gould abandon live performance?
items.why: Exact answer: 1964 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
